news_summary: Ancient hunter-gatherers demonstrated environmental impact and resource management by introducing fish to a mountain lake 7,000 years ago.
news_summary_fr: Il y a 7 000 ans, les anciens chasseurs-cueilleurs ont fait preuve d'une prise en compte de l'impact environnemental et d'une gestion des ressources en introduisant des poissons dans un lac de montagne.
news_summary_es: Los antiguos cazadores-recolectores demostraron su capacidad para gestionar el impacto medioambiental y los recursos al introducir peces en un lago de montaña hace 7.000 años.

articles.title: Hunter-gatherers introduced fish to a mountain lake 7000 years ago
articles.summary: Many centuries before the advent of agriculture in Norway, ancient people transported fish into mountain lakes to create a source of food, then built wooden traps to catch them
articles.content:
Many centuries before the advent of agriculture in Norway, ancient people transported fish into mountain lakes to create a source of food, then built wooden traps to catch them

The exposed bed of Lake Tesse in Norway, where archaeologists found ancient fish traps Magne Samdal, Museum of Cultural History, University of Oslo

Hunter-gatherers transported brown trout into the mountains of Norway at least 7000 years ago so they could be harvested during summer foraging trips into the highlands.

That is the only possible explanation for how fish could have populated isolated mountain lakes that had been covered by a kilometres-thick ice sheet for tens of thousands of years, says Axel Mjærum at the University of Oslo, Norway.

“This is the oldest documented example of human-mediated translocation of fish in the world,” says Mjærum.

Advertisement

Until around 8400 years ago, Lake Tesse, at an elevation of around 850 metres, was covered by the Fennoscandian ice sheet, leaving an ecological blank canvas once it retreated.

Yet before it was transformed for hydropower storage in the 1940s, Lake Tesse was regarded as one of the richest brown trout waterways in Norway, yielding 9 tonnes of the fish annually.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Sign up to newsletter

While it has long been suspected that humans had a hand in establishing the brown trout population in Lake Tesse, we had no idea how long ago this happened.

Alternative ideas have included transportation by birds or natural migration of fish and eggs upstream. But most Norwegian mountain lakes, including Lake Tesse, only had brown trout, and geographical features such as large waterfalls make natural dispersal upstream impossible.

Mjærum and his colleagues studied the partially buried remains of elaborate fish traps, discovered in 2022 when the lake level was lowered.

Radiocarbon dating showed that the oldest trap dated from around 4950 BC. By correlating the recovered wood with the pine tree ring chronology available for the central Scandinavian mountains, the team was also able to precisely date some of the younger posts.

One was felled between spring 2669 BC and winter 2669-2668 BC, and another 12 years later during the winter of 2657-2656 BC, demonstrating that the traps must have been maintained to keep them in good working order.

“Therefore, it is possible that this form of trap fishing was ongoing in Tesse during much of the period between 5000 and 2650 BC,” says Mjærum.

The transport of fish into the lakes is an early example of people managing the environment to provide food, centuries before the advent of farming in Scandinavia. There is evidence that fish farming began even earlier in other parts of the world, including channels and traps for catching eels built in Australia 8600 years ago.

How people transported the fish is unknown, but they may have used waterskins. People probably stayed in the mountains for part of the summer season, hunting reindeer and fishing, but spent the winters in the valleys or along the coast where the climate was milder, says Mjærum.

“A good and predictable food supply also made longer stays possible, not only for hunters aiming for big game, but for whole family groups,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery revealing that hunter-gatherers managed fish populations 7000 years ago, demonstrating early human environmental management and food resource innovation with broad anthropological and historical significance.
articles.personality_title: Ancient hunters stocked mountain lake with fish 7,000 years ago
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around 7,000 years ago, long before farming began in Norway, the land was still recovering from the last Ice Age. Mountain lakes were empty of fish because thick ice sheets had covered the area for thousands of years.

**What happened** – Archaeologists discovered that ancient hunter-gatherers moved brown trout into Lake Tesse, a high mountain lake in Norway. They built wooden fish traps to catch the trout during summer visits. Radiocarbon dating shows these traps were used between about 5000 BC and 2650 BC, meaning people maintained this fishing method for centuries.

**Impact** – This is the oldest known example in the world of humans moving fish to new places to create a food source. It shows that people managed their environment carefully long before farming started. The finding also explains how fish ended up in isolated mountain lakes where natural fish migration was impossible.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to learn more about how the fish were transported, possibly in waterskins, and how this early food management helped ancient communities live longer in the mountains. This discovery may inspire new studies on early human environmental practices worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Seven thousand years ago, hunter-gatherers in Norway moved and caught fish in mountain lakes, showing early human efforts to control nature for food well before farming began.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des chasseurs-cueilleurs ont introduit des poissons dans un lac de montagne il y a 7000 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ 7000 ans, bien avant le début de l'agriculture en Norvège, la région se remettait encore de la dernière période glaciaire. Les lacs de montagne étaient vides de poissons, car d'épais glaciers recouvraient la zone depuis des milliers d'années.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des archéologues ont découvert que des chasseurs-cueilleurs anciens avaient transporté des truites brunes dans le lac Tesse, un lac de haute montagne en Norvège. Ils construisaient des pièges en bois pour les attraper pendant leurs visites estivales. La datation au radiocarbone montre que ces pièges étaient utilisés entre environ 5000 av. J.-C. et 2650 av. J.-C., ce qui signifie que cette méthode de pêche était maintenue pendant des siècles.

**Impact** – C’est le plus ancien exemple connu au monde d’humains déplaçant des poissons pour créer une source de nourriture. Cela montre que les populations géraient leur environnement bien avant l’agriculture. Cette découverte explique aussi comment les poissons ont pu peupler des lacs isolés où la migration naturelle était impossible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent en savoir plus sur la manière dont les poissons ont été transportés, peut-être dans des outres, et sur la façon dont cette gestion alimentaire précoce a permis aux communautés anciennes de rester plus longtemps en montagne. Cette découverte pourrait encourager d’autres études sur les pratiques environnementales anciennes dans le monde.

**Une phrase clé** – Il y a 7000 ans, des chasseurs-cueilleurs norvégiens ont déplacé et pêché des poissons dans des lacs de montagne, montrant des efforts précoces pour contrôler la nature avant l’agriculture.
articles.personality_title_es: Cazadores-recolectores introdujeron peces en un lago de montaña hace 7,000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos 7,000 años, mucho antes de que comenzara la agricultura en Noruega, la tierra aún se recuperaba de la última Edad de Hielo. Los lagos de montaña estaban vacíos de peces porque una gruesa capa de hielo los cubría durante miles de años.

**Qué pasó** – Arqueólogos descubrieron que cazadores-recolectores antiguos trasladaron truchas marrones al lago Tesse, un lago de montaña en Noruega. Construyeron trampas de madera para pescar durante sus visitas de verano. Las dataciones por radiocarbono muestran que estas trampas se usaron entre aproximadamente 5000 a.C. y 2650 a.C., lo que indica que mantuvieron esta forma de pesca durante siglos.

**Impacto** – Este es el ejemplo más antiguo conocido en el mundo de personas moviendo peces a nuevos lugares para crear una fuente de alimento. Muestra que las personas gestionaban su entorno con cuidado mucho antes de la agricultura. También explica cómo los peces llegaron a lagos de montaña aislados donde la migración natural era imposible.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan aprender más sobre cómo transportaban los peces, posiblemente en pieles de agua, y cómo esta gestión temprana de alimentos ayudó a las comunidades antiguas a vivir más tiempo en las montañas. Este hallazgo podría inspirar nuevos estudios sobre prácticas ambientales humanas tempranas en todo el mundo.

**Frase clave** – Hace siete mil años, cazadores-recolectores en Noruega movieron y pescaron peces en lagos de montaña, mostrando esfuerzos humanos tempranos para controlar la naturaleza antes de la agricultura.
articles.image_prompt: A serene mountain lake at dawn with gentle ripples revealing several brown trout beneath the clear water, alongside intricately arranged wooden fish traps partially submerged near the shore, framed by silhouetted pine trees and distant snow-capped peaks under soft natural tones.
